revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/77325565|神鏡]]"
revision.text:
[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|台湾の[[Gaoshi Shrine|クスクス神社]]。あの神社の前にある鏡は神鏡である。]] '''神鏡'''（しんきょう）とは、神聖な鏡という意味の一般名詞である。神霊のご神体として神社の本殿に祀られている鏡もあれば、または拝殿の神前に置かれている鏡もある。[[Imperial Regalia of Japan|三種の神器]]の一つである[[Yata no Kagami|八咫の鏡]]も、神鏡の一つである。
== 意義 == 神鏡の意義に関しては、一般的には太陽を鏡で指していると言われる。これは、鏡で日の光を反射した際、それを正面から見ると太陽のように輝いて見える為であり、[[Shinto|日本神道]]では[[Solar deity|太陽神]]である[[Amaterasu|天照大神]]（アマテラスオオカミ）を最上の神として崇め祀るので、太陽を象徴する鏡で以て御[[Shintai|神体]]とし、神社に祀るとされている。『[[Nihon Shoki|日本書紀]]』においては、[[Amaterasu|天照大神]]は孫である[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|瓊瓊杵尊]]（ニニギノミコト）に、「これらの鏡を私の御魂として、拝するように常に奉りなさい」と記述されている。
The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.
== History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
なお、[[Korea|朝鮮]]においても神鏡は多数出土される。
兵庫県井石神社の神鏡は、新羅から帰化した天日槍が伝来したと伝える。一方、宮内省は、[[1895|1895年]]（[[1895|明治28年]]）以降創立の官国幣社に対し、御霊代は神鏡とする旨の通達をした。
== 神鏡の種類 ==
; 葉脈文鏡 : 中国王朝の内、[[Shang dynasty|殷]]（いん）・[[Zhou dynasty|周]]代のものであり、紀元前1600年~紀元前770年と世界最古の神鏡である。現在でも21面しか発見されておらず、銅鏡は簡素なものであるが、日本の土器によくある葉脈文や平行線文などの幾何学文様が見られる。
; 素面鏡 : 春秋戦国時代の、紀元前770年~紀元前220年の神鏡。外見は名前の通り素朴で平板なものであるが、薄くて軽い為に技術の向上が見られる。
; 蟠龍菱文鏡(はんりゅうひしもんきょう) : [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|前漢]]前期の、紀元前219年~紀元前111年の神鏡。とぐろを巻く[[Dragon|龍]]や蛇の図案が彫られている。
; 方格規矩四神鏡 : 1~2世紀後漢の神鏡。中央に方形の線が彫られ、それぞれの区画を[[Azure Dragon|青龍]]・[[White Tiger (mythology)|白虎]]・[[Vermilion Bird|朱雀]]・[[Black Tortoise|玄武]]の四神の文様が配置されている。
; 神仙画像鏡 : 2~3世紀の後漢の神鏡。文様の特徴として、[[Xian (Taoism)|神仙]]の[[Old Man of the South Pole|南極老人]]、天皇大帝、[[King Father of the East|東王父]]、[[Queen Mother of the West|西王母]]などが彫られている。
; 青龍三年銘方格規矩四神鏡 : 日本最古の神鏡。この神鏡の銘にある「青龍3年」が、中国の魏の年号で西暦235年に当たり、『魏志倭人伝』で[[Yamatai|邪馬台国]]の女王・[[Himiko|卑弥呼]]が[[Cao Wei|魏]]に遣いを送ったのが景初3年(239年)のことであり、帰国したのが正始元年(240年)であるので、その中にこの鏡が含まれていた可能性が指摘されている。
; 漢龍虎鏡 : 3世紀後漢晩期の神鏡。中央に龍と虎の文様が睨み合っている為にこう呼ばれる。
; 三角縁神獣鏡(さんかくえんしんじゅうきょう/さんかくぶちしんじゅうきょう) : 青銅で出来、断面が突出して三角形を成しており、且つ神獣の文様が刻まれている為にこう呼ばれる。日本でのみ出土されるが、神獣鏡は中国や朝鮮でも多数出土されており、三角縁である点が日本固有の特徴である。
: 日本で作られたものなのか、中国で作られたものなのか判然としておらず、卑弥呼が魏の皇帝から下賜された銅鏡百枚の内に含まれるのではないかと言われているが、邪馬台国の時代の3世紀の遺跡・墓からは全く出土しないで、4世紀の古墳時代の遺跡のみから出土されている。
; 御霊代神鏡 : [[Shinto shrine|神社]]や宮中では神鏡を[[Shintai|御霊代]]とする事が多い。規定では「天神地祇並びに[[Emperor of Japan|天皇]]はその鏡径を一尺、諸臣は七寸とし、裏面に神名を刻し、[[Button|鈕]]に紅[[Twine|紐]]をかけ、錦の[[Bag|袋]]に入れ、柳箱に納め、この箱を入帷子に包み、唐櫃に納め、ここに覆いをかけるか、御衾で包む。なお、唐櫃を使用せず、神鏡を[[Silk|絹]]で包み、御樋代に、次に御船代に納め、御衾で覆うも可」としている。
== 参考文献 ==
* 『宇佐八幡と古代神鏡の謎』(著者：田村円澄、木村晴彦・桃坂豊 版：戎光祥出版)

== 外部リンク ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ 神鏡美術館] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[Category:Mirrors]]

[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|台湾の[[Gaoshi Shrine|クスクス神社]]。あの神社の前にある鏡は神鏡である。]] '''神鏡'''（しんきょう）とは、神聖な鏡という意味の一般名詞である。神霊のご神体として神社の本殿に祀られている鏡もあれば、または拝殿の神前に置かれている鏡もある。[[Imperial Regalia of Japan|三種の神器]]の一つである[[Yata no Kagami|八咫の鏡]]も、神鏡の一つである。
== Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai|Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.
== History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
なお、[[Korea|朝鮮]]においても神鏡は多数出土される。
兵庫県井石神社の神鏡は、新羅から帰化した天日槍が伝来したと伝える。一方、宮内省は、[[1895|1895年]]（[[1895|明治28年]]）以降創立の官国幣社に対し、御霊代は神鏡とする旨の通達をした。

[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|台湾の[[Gaoshi Shrine|クスクス神社]]。あの神社の前にある鏡は神鏡である。]] '''Shinkyou''' is a general noun meaning a sacred mirror. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. The [[Yata no Kagami|Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.
== Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai|Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.
== History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
なお、[[Korea|朝鮮]]においても神鏡は多数出土される。
兵庫県井石神社の神鏡は、新羅から帰化した天日槍が伝来したと伝える。一方、宮内省は、[[1895|1895年]]（[[1895|明治28年]]）以降創立の官国幣社に対し、御霊代は神鏡とする旨の通達をした。

; 葉脈文鏡 : 中国王朝の内、[[Shang dynasty|殷]]（いん）・[[Zhou dynasty|周]]代のものであり、紀元前1600年~紀元前770年と世界最古の神鏡である。現在でも21面しか発見されておらず、銅鏡は簡素なものであるが、日本の土器によくある葉脈文や平行線文などの幾何学文様が見られる。

; 神仙画像鏡 : 2~3世紀の後漢の神鏡。文様の特徴として、[[Xian (Taoism)|神仙]]の[[Old Man of the South Pole|南極老人]]、天皇大帝、[[King Father of the East|東王父]]、[[Queen Mother of the West|西王母]]などが彫られている。
; 青龍三年銘方格規矩四神鏡 : 日本最古の神鏡。この神鏡の銘にある「青龍3年」が、中国の魏の年号で西暦235年に当たり、『魏志倭人伝』で[[Yamatai|邪馬台国]]の女王・[[Himiko|卑弥呼]]が[[Cao Wei|魏]]に遣いを送ったのが景初3年(239年)のことであり、帰国したのが正始元年(240年)であるので、その中にこの鏡が含まれていた可能性が指摘されている。
; 漢龍虎鏡 : 3世紀後漢晩期の神鏡。中央に龍と虎の文様が睨み合っている為にこう呼ばれる。
; 三角縁神獣鏡(さんかくえんしんじゅうきょう/さんかくぶちしんじゅうきょう) : 青銅で出来、断面が突出して三角形を成しており、且つ神獣の文様が刻まれている為にこう呼ばれる。日本でのみ出土されるが、神獣鏡は中国や朝鮮でも多数出土されており、三角縁である点が日本固有の特徴である。
: 日本で作られたものなのか、中国で作られたものなのか判然としておらず、卑弥呼が魏の皇帝から下賜された銅鏡百枚の内に含まれるのではないかと言われているが、邪馬台国の時代の3世紀の遺跡・墓からは全く出土しないで、4世紀の古墳時代の遺跡のみから出土されている。
; 御霊代神鏡 : [[Shinto shrine|神社]]や宮中では神鏡を[[Shintai|御霊代]]とする事が多い。規定では「天神地祇並びに[[Emperor of Japan|天皇]]はその鏡径を一尺、諸臣は七寸とし、裏面に神名を刻し、[[Button|鈕]]に紅[[Twine|紐]]をかけ、錦の[[Bag|袋]]に入れ、柳箱に納め、この箱を入帷子に包み、唐櫃に納め、ここに覆いをかけるか、御衾で包む。なお、唐櫃を使用せず、神鏡を[[Silk|絹]]で包み、御樋代に、次に御船代に納め、御衾で覆うも可」としている。
== References ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)

== 外部リンク ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ 神鏡美術館] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[Category:Mirrors]]

[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|台湾の[[Gaoshi Shrine|クスクス神社]]。あの神社の前にある鏡は神鏡である。]] '''Shinkyou''' is a general noun meaning a sacred mirror. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. The [[Yata no Kagami|Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.
== Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai|Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.
== History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
In [[Korea|Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated.
The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from Silla. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895|1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.
== 神鏡の種類 ==
; 葉脈文鏡 : 中国王朝の内、[[Shang dynasty|殷]]（いん）・[[Zhou dynasty|周]]代のものであり、紀元前1600年~紀元前770年と世界最古の神鏡である。現在でも21面しか発見されておらず、銅鏡は簡素なものであるが、日本の土器によくある葉脈文や平行線文などの幾何学文様が見られる。
; 素面鏡 : 春秋戦国時代の、紀元前770年~紀元前220年の神鏡。外見は名前の通り素朴で平板なものであるが、薄くて軽い為に技術の向上が見られる。
; 蟠龍菱文鏡(はんりゅうひしもんきょう) : [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|前漢]]前期の、紀元前219年~紀元前111年の神鏡。とぐろを巻く[[Dragon|龍]]や蛇の図案が彫られている。
; 方格規矩四神鏡 : 1~2世紀後漢の神鏡。中央に方形の線が彫られ、それぞれの区画を[[Azure Dragon|青龍]]・[[White Tiger (mythology)|白虎]]・[[Vermilion Bird|朱雀]]・[[Black Tortoise|玄武]]の四神の文様が配置されている。
; 神仙画像鏡 : 2~3世紀の後漢の神鏡。文様の特徴として、[[Xian (Taoism)|神仙]]の[[Old Man of the South Pole|南極老人]]、天皇大帝、[[King Father of the East|東王父]]、[[Queen Mother of the West|西王母]]などが彫られている。
; 青龍三年銘方格規矩四神鏡 : 日本最古の神鏡。この神鏡の銘にある「青龍3年」が、中国の魏の年号で西暦235年に当たり、『魏志倭人伝』で[[Yamatai|邪馬台国]]の女王・[[Himiko|卑弥呼]]が[[Cao Wei|魏]]に遣いを送ったのが景初3年(239年)のことであり、帰国したのが正始元年(240年)であるので、その中にこの鏡が含まれていた可能性が指摘されている。
; 漢龍虎鏡 : 3世紀後漢晩期の神鏡。中央に龍と虎の文様が睨み合っている為にこう呼ばれる。
; 三角縁神獣鏡(さんかくえんしんじゅうきょう/さんかくぶちしんじゅうきょう) : 青銅で出来、断面が突出して三角形を成しており、且つ神獣の文様が刻まれている為にこう呼ばれる。日本でのみ出土されるが、神獣鏡は中国や朝鮮でも多数出土されており、三角縁である点が日本固有の特徴である。
: 日本で作られたものなのか、中国で作られたものなのか判然としておらず、卑弥呼が魏の皇帝から下賜された銅鏡百枚の内に含まれるのではないかと言われているが、邪馬台国の時代の3世紀の遺跡・墓からは全く出土しないで、4世紀の古墳時代の遺跡のみから出土されている。
; 御霊代神鏡 : [[Shinto shrine|神社]]や宮中では神鏡を[[Shintai|御霊代]]とする事が多い。規定では「天神地祇並びに[[Emperor of Japan|天皇]]はその鏡径を一尺、諸臣は七寸とし、裏面に神名を刻し、[[Button|鈕]]に紅[[Twine|紐]]をかけ、錦の[[Bag|袋]]に入れ、柳箱に納め、この箱を入帷子に包み、唐櫃に納め、ここに覆いをかけるか、御衾で包む。なお、唐櫃を使用せず、神鏡を[[Silk|絹]]で包み、御樋代に、次に御船代に納め、御衾で覆うも可」としている。

[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|台湾の[[Gaoshi Shrine|クスクス神社]]。あの神社の前にある鏡は神鏡である。]] '''Shinkyou''' is a general noun meaning a sacred mirror. == History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
In [[Korea|Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated.
The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from Silla. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895|1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.
== Types of Shinto Mirrors ==
; Mirror with foliate pattern : This is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware.
Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen. : A divine mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.
Coiled dragon with diamond design : 蟠龍菱文鏡 : [[Han Dynasty#Western Han|Former Han]], 219 BC - 111 BC. It is engraved with designs of [[Dragon]]s and serpents spinning in circles.
Mirror of the Four Gods : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird|Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].
; Divine Immortal Image Mirror : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese divine mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.
Mirror of the Four Gods This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko|Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.
Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.
This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.
Shinto shrine : [[Shinto shrine In the : 当該(とうがい)the : 当該(とうがい)the [[Shinto shrines]] and : 及び(および), かつ(かつ), 並びに(ならびに) / A and B, C, and Dand in the : 当該(とうがい)the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]].
== References ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)

The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko|Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.
This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.
Shinto shrine : [[Shinto shrine In the : 当該(とうがい)the : 当該(とうがい)the [[Shinto shrines]] and : 及び(および), かつ(かつ), 並びに(ならびに) / A and B, C, and Dand in the : 当該(とうがい)the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]].
== References ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)

[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|台湾の[[Gaoshi Shrine|クスクス神社]]。あの神社の前にある鏡は神鏡である。]] '''Shinkyou''' is a general noun meaning a sacred mirror. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. The [[Yata no Kagami|Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.
== Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai|Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.
== History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
In [[Korea|Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated.
The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from Silla. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895|1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.
== Types of Shinto Mirrors ==
=== Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.
=== Somen mirror === : A divine mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.
=== Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved.
=== Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird|Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].
=== Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese divine mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.
=== The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.
=== Goryo Shintai === Shinto shrine : [[Shinto shrine In the : 当該(とうがい)the : 当該(とうがい)the [[Shinto shrines]] and : 及び(および), かつ(かつ), 並びに(ならびに) / A and B, C, and Dand in the : 当該(とうがい)the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]].
== References ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)

In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.
== History of Shinkyo == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.
In [[Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated.
The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from Silla. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of Shinto Mirrors ==
=== Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.
=== Somen mirror === : A divine mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.
=== Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved.
=== Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].
=== Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese divine mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.
=== The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.
=== Goryo Shintai === In many shrines and palaces, the divine mirror is used as a sacred object. In the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]].<ref>"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== References ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)

[[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|The [[Gaoshi Shrine]] in Taiwan. The mirror in front of that shrine is a divine mirror.]] '''Shinkyou''' is a general noun meaning a sacred mirror. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.

In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform.

In [[Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated.

On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>

The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.

The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.
=== Goryo Shintai === In many shrines and palaces, the divine mirror is used as a sacred object. In the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]].<ref>"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== References ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== External links ==

revision.text: {{AFC submission|d|nn|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=ReaderofthePack|declinets=20220211160335|ts=20220209175934}} {{AFC comment|1=This needs sourcing to establish how this is notable, as well as improvements to the claims. Claims also need to be attributed. For example, when writing "some say" the question is who is saying this? Is anyone actually saying this? If so, then what are their qualifications? Keep in mind that we can only summarize what has been explicitly stated in reliable sources, preferably academic and scholarly sources. [[User:ReaderofthePack|ReaderofthePack]](formerly Tokyogirl79)[[User talk:ReaderofthePack|''' (｡◕‿◕｡)''']] 16:03, 11 February 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} [[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|The [[Gaoshi Shrine]] in Taiwan. The mirror in front of that shrine is a divine mirror.]] A Shinto mirror is a mirror sacred in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors. == Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform. == History of Shinto mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China. In [[Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated. The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from Silla. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行 == Types of Shinto Mirrors == === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen. === Somen mirror === : A divine mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology. === Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved. === Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]]. === Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese divine mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others. === The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them. === Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center. === Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well. It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period. === Goryo Shintai === In many shrines and palaces, the divine mirror is used as a sacred object. In the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]]."Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951 == References == * "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan) == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == External links == * [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] [[:Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[:Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[:Category:Mirrors]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

The mirror in front of that shrine is a divine mirror.]] A {{Interlanguage link|Shinto mirror|ja|神鏡}} is a mirror sacred in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence (being uncommon items). [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}} The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318-326|language=en}} == Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform. == History of Shinto mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", Himiko, the queen of the Jumadai Kingdom, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China. In [[Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated, including perhaps {{Interlanguage link|Tachusaimonkyo|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from Silla. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行 == Types of Shinto Mirrors == === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen. === Somen mirror === : A divine mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology. === Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved. === Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]]. === Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese divine mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others. === The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them. === Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center. === Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well. It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period. === Goryo Shintai === In many shrines and palaces, the divine mirror is used as a sacred object. In the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]]."Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951 == See Also == * [[Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] == References == * "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan) == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == External links == * [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] [[:Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[:Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[:Category:Mirrors]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

{{AFC submission|d|nn|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=ReaderofthePack|declinets=20220211160335|ts=20220209175934}} {{AFC comment|1=It's not that I think any of this content is necessarily wrong, just that sourcing and attribution of claims is important. [[User:ReaderofthePack|ReaderofthePack]](formerly Tokyogirl79)[[User talk:ReaderofthePack|''' (｡◕‿◕｡)''']] 16:04, 11 February 2022 (UTC)}} {{AFC comment|1=This needs sourcing to establish how this is notable, as well as improvements to the claims. Claims also need to be attributed. For example, when writing "some say" the question is who is saying this? Is anyone actually saying this? If so, then what are their qualifications? Keep in mind that we can only summarize what has been explicitly stated in reliable sources, preferably academic and scholarly sources. [[User:ReaderofthePack|ReaderofthePack]](formerly Tokyogirl79)[[User talk:ReaderofthePack|''' (｡◕‿◕｡)''']] 16:03, 11 February 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} [[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|The [[Gaoshi Shrine]] in Taiwan. The mirror in front of that shrine is a divine mirror.]] A {{Interlanguage link|Shinto mirror|ja|神鏡}} is a mirror sacred in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship. Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence (being uncommon items). [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}} The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318-326|language=en}} == Significance == As for the significance of the Shinto mirror, it is generally said that it refers to the sun in a mirror. In [[Shintoism]], [[Amaterasu]], the [[Solar deity]], is worshipped and enshrined as the supreme deity. It is said that the mirror symbolizing the sun is used as the [[Shintai]] and enshrined in the shrine. In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this divine mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform. == History of Shinto mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four Divine Mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C. In addition, according to the "[[wajinden]]", [[Himiko]], the queen of the [[Yamatai|Yamatai Kingdom]], sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China. In [[Korea]], a number of divine mirrors have also been excavated, including perhaps {{Interlanguage link|Tachusaimonkyo|ja|多鈕細文鏡}} The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from [[Silla]]. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行 == Types of Shinto Mirrors == === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest divine mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen. === Somen mirror === : A divine mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology. === Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved. === Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]]. === Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese divine mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others. === The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them. === Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center. === Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well. It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period. === Goryo Shintai === In many shrines and palaces, the divine mirror is used as a sacred object. In the Imperial Court, divine mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]]."Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951 == See Also == * [[Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] == References == * "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan) == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == External links == * [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] [[:Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[:Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[:Category:Mirrors]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]]."Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951 == See Also == * [[Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] == Bibliography == * "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan) == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == * [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] [[:Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[:Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[:Category:Mirrors]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

revision.text: {{AFC submission|d|nn|u=MaitreyaVaruna|ns=118|decliner=ReaderofthePack|declinets=20220211160335|ts=20220209175934}} {{AFC comment|1=It's not that I think any of this content is necessarily wrong, just that sourcing and attribution of claims is important. [[User:ReaderofthePack|ReaderofthePack]](formerly Tokyogirl79)[[User talk:ReaderofthePack|''' (｡◕‿◕｡)''']] 16:04, 11 February 2022 (UTC)}} {{AFC comment|1=This needs sourcing to establish how this is notable, as well as improvements to the claims. Claims also need to be attributed. For example, when writing "some say" the question is who is saying this? Is anyone actually saying this? If so, then what are their qualifications? Keep in mind that we can only summarize what has been explicitly stated in reliable sources, preferably academic and scholarly sources. [[User:ReaderofthePack|ReaderofthePack]](formerly Tokyogirl79)[[User talk:ReaderofthePack|''' (｡◕‿◕｡)''']] 16:03, 11 February 2022 (UTC)}} ---- {{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} [[File:高士神社.jpeg|thumb|The [[Gaoshi Shrine]] in Taiwan. The mirror in front of that shrine is a shinto mirror.]] A {{Interlanguage link|Shinto mirror|ja|神鏡}} {{Nihongo|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a mirror sacred in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}. Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence (being uncommon items){{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}. [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}} The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318-326|language=en}} == Significance == Spirits are enshrined in Shinto mirrors as [[Shintai]]{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}} In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Always worship these mirrors as my soul. The platform on which this shinto mirror is placed is usually in the shape of a cloud, and is called a cloud-shaped platform".{{Cite book |last=1901-1987 |first=坂本太郎, |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=2003.9-2003.11 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} == History of Shinto mirrors == It is said that the origin of the shinto mirror dates back to China. In China, more ancient shinto mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four shinto mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest shinto mirror in China is the "Mirror with a leaf vein design," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "[[wajinden]]", [[Himiko]], the queen of the [[Yamatai|Yamatai Kingdom]], sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's shinto mirror culture is a cultural import from China. The Shinto mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyogo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from [[Silla]]. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[1895]] ([[1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be Shinto mirrors.『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行 == Types of Shinto Mirrors == === Goryo Shintai === In many shrines and palaces, the shinto mirror is used as a sacred object. In the Imperial Court, shinto mirrors are often referred to as [[Shintai|Goryoyo]]. According to the regulations, "The [[Shinto shrine]]s, [[Emperor of Japan]]s, and [[Tenjin]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of one shaku, and [[Emperor of Japan]]s shall have a mirror with a diameter of seven shaku. This box is then wrapped in [[Mail]] and placed in a [[Karabitsu]]. It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]]."Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951 == See Also == * [[Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] == Bibliography == * "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient Shinto Mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan) == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == * [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] [[:Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[:Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[:Category:Mirrors]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

It is also possible to wrap the mirror in [[silk]] and place it in the [[Ohiyo]], then in the [[Funashiyo]], and cover it with the [[Goshiki]]."Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951 == See Also == * [[Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]] == Bibliography == * "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient divine mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan) == References == {{Reflist}} == External links == * [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] [[:Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[:Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[:Category:Shinto]] [[:Category:Shinto cult objects]] [[:Category:Mirrors]] {{Drafts moved from mainspace|date=February 2022}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591009]] Wikidata name: Shinto mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "[[:en:Wajinden|Wajinden]]", [[Himiko]], the queen of the [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai Kingdom]], sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]
== Bibliography ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient divine mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071011181517/http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ |date=2007-10-11 }}
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Mirrors]] [[Category:Bronze mirrors]]
}}
[[en:Mirrors in Shinto]]
<!-- === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Somen mirror === : A shinto mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese shinto mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of [[:en:Yamatai|Umataikoku]], [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}-->

Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591009]] Wikidata name: Shinto mirror [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Three Sacred Treasures]], is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "[[:en:Wajinden|Wajinden]]", [[Himiko]], the queen of the [[:en:Yamatai|Yamatai Kingdom]], sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after [[:en:1895|1895]] ([[:en:1895|28th year of Meiji]]) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]] * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]
== Bibliography ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient divine mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071011181517/http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ |date=2007-10-11 }}
