== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]

<!-- === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Somen mirror === : A shinto mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}

Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures}}, is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}} * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]
== Bibliography ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient divine mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071011181517/http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ |date=2007-10-11 }}
{{Kofun navbar}}
[[en:Mirrors in Shinto]]
<!-- === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Somen mirror === : A shinto mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese shinto mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Umataikoku}}, [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mirrors in Shintō}} [[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]] [[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]] [[id:Cermin Shinto]] [[ja:神鏡]] {{wikidata link|Q11591009}} [[Category:Bronze mirrors]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Mirrors]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

{{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}, is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom|1=Yamatai}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>

Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom|1=Yamatai}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]
== Bibliography ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient divine mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)

== External links ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071011181517/http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ |date=2007-10-11 }}
{{Kofun navbar}}
[[en:Mirrors in Shinto]]
<!-- === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Somen mirror === : A shinto mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese shinto mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Umataikoku|1=Yamatai}}, [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mirrors in Shintō}} [[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]] [[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]] [[id:Cermin Shinto]] [[ja:神鏡]] {{wikidata link|Q11591009}} [[Category:Bronze mirrors]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Mirrors]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]]
{{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}, is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom|1=Yamatai}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>

It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mirrors in Shintō}} [[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]] [[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]] [[id:Cermin Shinto]] [[ja:神鏡]] {{wikidata link|Q11591009}} [[Category:Bronze mirrors]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Mirrors]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>

In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />

The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>

=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>

<!-- === Mirror with foliate pattern === : This is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C., and belonging to the [[Shang dynasty]] and [[Zhou dynasty]] of the Chinese dynasty. It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Somen mirror === : A shinto mirror from 770 B.C. to 220 B.C. during the Spring and Autumn Warring States Period. As its name suggests, it is simple and flat in appearance, but its thinness and lightness show the improvement of technology.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Hanryu Hishimonkyo === :: A mirror from 219 BC to 111 BC in the early [[Western Han]] period. Designs of dragons and snakes that wind around the tongue are engraved.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Mirror of the Four Gods === : 1st~2nd century Later Han Dynasty mirror. A square line is carved in the center, and each section is decorated with the designs of four gods: [[Azure Dragon|Blue Dragon]], [[White Tiger (mythology)|White Tiger]], [[Vermilion Bird]], and [[Black Tortoise|Genbu]].{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Divine Immortal Image Mirror === : A 2nd~3rd century Later Han Chinese shinto mirror. The design features carvings of [[Xian (Taoism)|Shen Xian]], [[Old Man of the South Pole|The Old Man of the South Pole]], the Emperor of China, [[King Father of the East|Father of the East]], [[Queen Mother of the West|Mother of the West]], and others.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== The Three-year Inscription of Qinglong's Square and the Four Magic Mirrors === This is the oldest mirror in Japan. This mirror is the oldest of its kind in Japan. The year "Seiryu 3" in the inscription of this mirror corresponds to the year 235 AD in the Chinese Wei Dynasty, and in the "Wei Shi Wajin Den", the queen of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Umataikoku|1=Yamatai}}, [[Himiko]], sent an envoy to [[Cao Wei|Wei]] in the third year of Jingzhuang (239), and returned to Japan in the first year of Shoshi (240). It has been pointed out that this mirror may have been among them.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Chinese Dragon and Tiger Mirror === : Late 3rd-century Han mirror. It is called this because of the dragon and tiger patterns staring at each other in the center.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
=== Triangular Edge God Beast Mirror === This mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular rim. This type of mirror is made of bronze and has a triangular cross-section with protruding triangles and inscriptions of sacred animals. This type of mirror is found only in Japan, but there are many examples in China and Korea as well.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}
It is said to be one of the 100 bronze mirrors given to Himiko by the Wei emperor, but it has not been found in any 3rd century ruins or tombs during the period of the Yamataikoku, but only in the 4th century Kofun period.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mirrors in Shintō}} [[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]] [[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]] [[id:Cermin Shinto]] [[ja:神鏡]] {{wikidata link|Q11591009}} [[Category:Bronze mirrors]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Mirrors]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mirrors in Shintō]][[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Mirrors in Shinto]][[fa:آیینه مقدس (شینتو)]][[fr:Miroirs dans le shintoïsme]][[id:Cermin Shinto]][[ja:神鏡]][[simple:Mirrors in Shinto]]
{{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}, is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|WD=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|WD=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom|1=Yamatai}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|WD=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
== See also ==
* {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}} * [[Yata no Kagami]] * [[Suda Hachiman Shrine Mirror]]
== Bibliography ==
* "Usa Hachiman and the Mysteries of Ancient divine mirrors" (Author: Enzumi Tamura, Haruhiko Kimura, Yutaka Momosaka Edition: Ebisu Kosho Shuppan)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External links ==
* [http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ Kagami Art Museum] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071011181517/http://we.magma.jp/~ark/sinkyou_utyuu/honkan/ |date=2007-10-11 }}
{{Kofun navbar}}

It is the oldest shinto mirror in the world, dating from 1600 B.C.~770 B.C. Even today, only 21 mirrors have been found, and although the bronze mirrors are simple, they show geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware. Geometric patterns such as leaf vein patterns and parallel lines, which are common in Japanese earthenware, can be seen.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}}

{{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}, is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom|1=Yamatai}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|qid=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|qid=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Sacred mirrors used in Shinto}}
[[File:Fukakusa Kaidoguchicho, Fushimi Ward, Kyoto, Kyoto Prefecture 612-0805, Japan - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|A mirror on an altar at [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]].]] A {{Nihongo|'''Shinto mirror'''|2=神鏡|3=Shinkyou}} is a sacred mirror in [[Shinto]]. Some mirrors are enshrined in the main hall of a shrine as a sacred object of the divine spirit, or are placed in front of the deity in a hall of worship.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a" /> Mirrors in ancient Japan represented truth because they merely reflected what was shown, and were a source of much mystique and reverence.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2019-01-06 |title=Yata No Kagami - The Sacred Mirror of Japan {{!}} Suki Desu |url=https://skdesu.com/en/yata-no-kagami-the-sacred-mirror-of-japan/ |access-date=2022-05-09 |language=en-US}}</ref> [[Japanese folklore]] is rich in stories of life before mirrors were commonplace.<ref name="degaris-2013a">{{Cite book|last=De_Garis|first=Frederick|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hUm0AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA237|title=We Japanese|date=2013-09-05|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-136-18367-6|pages=237|language=en}}</ref>
The [[Yata no Kagami]], one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Three Sacred Treasures|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}, is also one of the sacred mirrors.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Denney|first=John W.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9ntpAwAAQBAJ&pg=PA321|title=Respect and Consideration|date=2011|publisher=Lulu.com|isbn=978-0-9568798-0-6|pages=321, 318–326|language=en}}</ref>
== Significance == Spirits are enshrined in divine mirrors as [[Shintai]]<ref>{{Cite web |last=日本国語大辞典,デジタル大辞泉,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 |title=神鏡とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E7%A5%9E%E9%8F%A1-536961 |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref> Mirrors are believed to have been used to reflect sunlight during [[:en:Sun-Worship|Sun-Worship]] creating optical effects<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mirrors may have worked magic in ancient Japanese rituals |url=http://www.kaogu.cn/en/International_exchange/Academic_activities___/2014/0207/45209.html |access-date=2022-05-09 |website=www.kaogu.cn}}</ref>
In the [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Amaterasu]] tells her grandson, [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]], "Take this and revere it as if it were myself".<ref>{{Cite book |author=坂本太郎 |url=http://worldcat.org/oclc/123050747 |title=日本書紀 |date=September–November 2003 |publisher=Iwanami shoten |isbn=4-00-007230-7 |oclc=123050747}}</ref>{{Page needed|date=May 2022}} <ref name="green-shinto-2012a">{{Cite web |date=2012-02-17 |title=The circular mirror: Shinto symbol |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/02/18/the-circular-mirror-shinto-symbol/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref>
It is believed a mirror helps a believer see a true image of themselves and their devotion<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-02-04 |title=The Magic Mirror Maker |url=https://www.kyotojournal.org/renewal/the-magic-mirror-maker/ |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=Kyoto Journal |language=en-US}}</ref> and worshipping the divine within themselves.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
Ancient Chinese theologians believed the soul to be a circular disk, so the usage of a circular mirror was seen as reflecting this.<ref name="green-shinto-2012a" />
== History of divine mirrors == It is said that the origin of the divine mirror dates back to China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" /> In China, more ancient divine mirrors have been unearthed than in Japan, and compared to the oldest mirror in Japan, the "Four divine mirrors with a rectangular shape inscribed in the third year of Seiryu," which is dated to 235 A.D., the oldest divine mirror in China is the "{{Nihongo|Leaf Vein Mirror|葉脈文鏡|Yōmyaku bun kagami}}," which is an order of magnitude older than Japan, dating from 1600 to 770 B.C.{{Citation needed|date=March 2022}} In addition, according to the "{{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}", [[Himiko]], the queen of the {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai Kingdom|1=Yamatai}}, sent an envoy to Wei and received a hundred bronze mirrors from Wei, suggesting that Japan's divine mirror culture is a cultural import from China.<ref name="degaris-2013a" />
The divine mirror at Iishi Shrine in Hyōgo Prefecture is said to have been introduced by a naturalized celestial spear from {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. On the other hand, the Ministry of the Imperial Household issued a notice to official national shrines founded after {{ill|1895|en|1895|qid=Q7844|lt=1895|1=1895}} ({{ill|1895|en|1895|qid=Q7844|lt=28th year of Meiji|1=1895}}) that their sacred objects should be divine mirrors.<ref>『神社有職故実』全１２９頁７頁昭和２６年７月１５日神社本庁発行</ref>
== Types of divine mirrors ==
=== Goryo Shintai === In Shinto shrines and the imperial court, mirrors are often used as [[Shintai]]. The regulations state, "The diameter of the mirror shall be 1 shaku for the heavenly deities and emperors, and 7 shaku for the nobles. The name of the deity shall be engraved on the reverse side, a red cord shall be attached to a knob, and it shall be placed in a brocade pouch. The mirror may be wrapped in silk and placed in the hidai, then in the funadai, and then covered with the bedding.<ref name="shinto-shrine-yushoku-kokusitsu-1951a">"Shinto Shrine Yushoku Kokusitsu", p. 129, 7, July 1, 1951. Published by the Jinja Main Office on July 1, 1951</ref>
